revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]（旧[[群馬県]][[群馬町]]）にある[[天台宗]]の寺院。山号は三鈷山。...'
revision.text:
'''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]（旧[[群馬県]][[群馬町]]）にある[[天台宗]]の寺院。山号は三鈷山。院号は吉祥院。本尊は[[釈迦如来]]。
==歴史== この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられ、[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。
==文化財== *高崎市指定文化財 **妙見社本殿
==所在地== *群馬県高崎市引間町213
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/shisho/gunma/gaiyo/bunkazai.htm 高崎市文化財（旧群馬町）]
{{DEFAULTSORT:みようけんし}} [[Category:群馬県の寺]] [[Category:天台系仏教]] [[Category:高崎市]]

==歴史== この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられ、[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。
==文化財== *高崎市指定文化財 **妙見社本殿
==所在地== *群馬県高崎市引間町213
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/shisho/gunma/gaiyo/bunkazai.htm 高崎市文化財（旧群馬町）]
{{DEFAULTSORT:みようけんし}} [[Category:天台宗]] [[Category:群馬県の寺]] [[Category:高崎市]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年5月}} '''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]引間町にある[[天台宗]]の[[寺院]]である。山号は三鈷山。院号は吉祥院。本尊は[[釈迦如来]]。
==歴史== この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられ、[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。

{{DEFAULTSORT:みようけんし}} [[Category:天台宗の寺院]] [[Category:群馬県の寺]] [[Category:高崎市の歴史]] [[Category:高崎市の建築物]]

{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 妙見寺 |画像 = File:myokenji.jpg|250px |所在地 = 群馬県高崎市引間町213 |山号 = 三鈷山 |宗派 = 天台宗 |本尊 = 釈迦如来 |創建年 = 715年 |開基 = 藤原忠明 |別称 = 七星山息災寺、羊妙見 |札所等 = |文化財 = 妙見社本殿 |}} '''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]引間町にある[[天台宗]]の[[寺院]]である。山号は三鈷山。院号は吉祥院。本尊は[[釈迦如来]]。
==歴史==
この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられる。
「花園星神記」によれば、忠明がこの寺へ来て宿泊し、夜半目覚めてあたりを見渡すと、乾の方角に光明が立ち上っていた。不思議に思って侍臣を遣わせ調べると、冷泉町の小祝池からとわかり、水底を探らせたところ、目が赤く首の白い珍しい亀を得た。これを時の帝に献じたところ喜ばれ、元号が霊亀に改められたという。
[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。羊妙見とも呼ばれ、[[多胡碑]]の「羊」との関連を指摘する向きもある。
[[平将門の乱]]では、染谷川の戦いで苦境に追い込まれた[[平良文]]が不思議な声を聞き、導かれてついて行ったところ当寺にたどり着いた。そこで僧から七星剣を渡され、以来[[妙見菩薩]]の加護を受けるようになった。良文と関係の深い[[秩父神社]]、[[千葉神社]]の妙見は当寺から勧請されたものである。
[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|入口 myokenji3.jpg|染谷川 myokenji4.jpg|境内 </gallery>

{{DEFAULTSORT:みようけんし}} [[Category:天台宗の寺院]] [[Category:群馬県の寺]] [[Category:高崎市の歴史]] [[Category:高崎市の建築物]]

{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 妙見寺 |画像 = File:myokenji.jpg|250px |所在地 = 群馬県高崎市引間町213 |山号 = 三鈷山 |宗派 = 天台宗 |本尊 = 釈迦如来 |創建年 = 715年 |開基 = 藤原忠明 |別称 = 七星山息災寺、羊妙見 |札所等 = |文化財 = 妙見社本殿 |}} '''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]引間町にある[[天台宗]]の[[寺院]]である。山号は三鈷山。院号は吉祥院。本尊は[[釈迦如来]]。
==歴史==
この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられる。
「花園星神記」によれば、忠明がこの寺へ来て宿泊し、夜半目覚めてあたりを見渡すと、乾の方角に光明が立ち上っていた。不思議に思って侍臣を遣わせ調べると、冷泉町の小祝池からとわかり、水底を探らせたところ、目が赤く首の白い珍しい亀を得た。これを時の帝に献じたところ喜ばれ、元号が霊亀に改められたという。
[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。羊妙見とも呼ばれ、[[多胡碑]]の「羊」との関連を指摘する向きもある。
[[平将門の乱]]では、染谷川の戦いで苦境に追い込まれた[[平良文]]が不思議な声を聞き、導かれてついて行ったところ当寺にたどり着いた。そこで僧から七星剣を渡され、以来[[妙見菩薩]]の加護を受けるようになった。良文と関係の深い[[秩父神社]]、[[千葉神社]]の妙見は当寺から勧請されたものである。
[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|入口 myokenji3.jpg|染谷川 myokenji4.jpg|境内 </gallery>

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Myōken Faith articles that need translation]] to [[Category:original pages]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: making drafts attribute to me
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. revision.comment: Removing this excess
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = qq.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = qq.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = qq.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, granting it official recognition and support.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, granting it official recognition and support.

'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."

revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, compiled in [[Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tako Kō Monument|ja|多胡碑}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Jōhei-Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|ja|平良文}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|ja|千葉神社}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, granting it official recognition and support.

19 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町) up to rev 90308691
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 妙見寺 |画像 = File:myokenji.jpg|250px |所在地 = 群馬県高崎市引間町213 | 緯度度 = 36| 緯度分 = 23| 緯度秒 = 35.2 | 経度度 = 139| 経度分 = 01| 経度秒 = 11.8 |山号 = 三鈷山 |宗派 = 天台宗 |本尊 = 釈迦如来 |創建年 = 715年 |開基 = 藤原忠明 |別称 = 七星山息災寺、羊妙見 |札所等 = |文化財 = 妙見社本殿 |地図2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} }} '''妙見寺'''（みょうけんじ）は、[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]引間町にある[[天台宗]]の[[寺院]]である。山号は三鈷山。院号は吉祥院。本尊は[[釈迦如来]]。
==歴史==
この寺の創建年代等については不詳であるが、[[714年]]（[[和銅]]7年）またはその翌年の[[715年]]（[[霊亀]]元年）[[上野国]][[大掾]]藤原忠明の開基により創建されたと伝えられる。
「花園星神記」によれば、忠明がこの寺へ来て宿泊し、夜半目覚めてあたりを見渡すと、乾の方角に光明が立ち上っていた。不思議に思って侍臣を遣わせ調べると、冷水町の小祝池からとわかり、水底を探らせたところ、目が赤く首の白い珍しい亀を得た。これを時の帝に献じたところ喜ばれ、元号が霊亀に改められたという。
[[797年]]（[[延暦]]16年）に成立した「[[続日本紀]]」にも「妙見寺」に関する記載があることから、古くからある寺院と考えられている。古くは「七星山息災寺」と号し、[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る寺として信仰されてきた。羊妙見とも呼ばれ、[[多胡碑]]の「羊」との関連を指摘する向きもある。
[[承平天慶の乱#平将門の乱|平将門の乱]]では、染谷川の戦いで苦境に追い込まれた[[平良文]]が不思議な声を聞き、導かれてついて行ったところ当寺にたどり着いた。そこで僧から七星剣を渡され、以来[[妙見菩薩]]の加護を受けるようになった。良文と関係の深い[[秩父神社]]、[[千葉神社]]の妙見は当寺から勧請されたものである。
[[江戸時代]]には[[江戸幕府]]から[[朱印状]]が与えられている。
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|入口 myokenji3.jpg|染谷川 myokenji4.jpg|境内 </gallery>
==文化財== *高崎市指定文化財 **妙見社本殿
==アクセス== *JR前橋駅から群馬温泉行きバス 「妙見前」下車
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ 妙見社本殿] - 高崎市
{{DEFAULTSORT:みようけんし たかさきしひきま}} [[Category:天台宗の寺院]] [[Category:群馬県の寺]] [[Category:高崎市の歴史]] [[Category:高崎市の建築物]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11447022]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|qid=Q855964}} |image = File:Myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} | prefecture= {{ill|Takasaki|qid=Q336438}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.39311111111111|N|139.01994444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[:en:Reiki|Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]].
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], compiled in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the [[:en:Tago Stele|Tako Kō Monument]], particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Jōhei-Tengyō War]], [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site
==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}
==References== {{reflist}}

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11447022]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}} |image = File:Myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} | prefecture= {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.39311111111111|N|139.01994444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[:en:Reiki|Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]].
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], compiled in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the [[:en:Tago Stele|Tako Kō Monument]], particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Jōhei-Tengyō War]], [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.

'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[:en:Reiki|Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]].
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], compiled in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the [[:en:Tago Stele|Tako Kō Monument]], particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Jōhei-Tengyō War]], [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.

There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11447022]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}} |image = File:Myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} | prefecture= {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.39311111111111|N|139.01994444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]] temple located in [[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or [[:en:Reiki|Reiki]] 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]].
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], compiled in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the [[:en:Tago Stele|Tako Kō Monument]], particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Jōhei-Tengyō War]], [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.

The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.

According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], compiled in [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the [[:en:Tago Stele|Tako Kō Monument]], particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the [[:en:Tengyō no Ran|Jōhei-Tengyō War]], [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at [[:en:Chichibu Shrine|Chichibu Shrine]] and [[:en:Chiba Shrine|Chiba Shrine]] were enshrined from this temple.
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>

== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)]]
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}} |image = File:Myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} | prefecture= {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.39311111111111|N|139.01994444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or {{ill|Reiki|en|Reiki|WD=Q183057|lt=Reiki}} 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, compiled in {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bosatsu}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tako Kō Monument}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Jōhei-Tengyō War}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|en|Taira no Yoshifumi|WD=Q11483528|lt=Taira no Yoshifumi}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|en|Chichibu Shrine|WD=Q2963366|lt=Chichibu Shrine}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|en|Chiba Shrine|WD=Q11406700|lt=Chiba Shrine}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōken-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964|1=Tendai}} |image = File:Myokenji.jpg|250px | location = 213 Hikima-machi, Takasaki City, Gunma Prefecture, Japan | latd = 36 |latm = 23 |lats = 35.2 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 01 |longs = 11.8 |longEW = E | mount = Sankosan | sect = Tendai | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = 715 | founder = Fujiwara no Tadaaki | alias = Shichiseizan Sokusaiji, Hitsuji Myōken | cultural_properties = Myōken-sha Main Hall | map2 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=12}} | prefecture= {{ill|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|1=Takasaki}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.39311111111111|N|139.01994444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Myōken-ji''' (妙見寺) is a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tiantai}} temple located in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Sankosan, and its cloister name (''ingō'') is Kisshōin. The temple's principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}.
== History == The founding date of this temple is unclear, but it is traditionally said to have been established in either [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 7 (714) or {{ill|Reiki|en|Reiki|WD=Q183057|lt=Reiki|1=Reiki}} 1 (715) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja|藤原忠明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tadaaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a local official in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}.
According to the "Hanazono Star God Record" (花園星神記), Tadaaki once stayed at the temple. Awakening at midnight, he noticed a bright light rising in the northwest direction. Curious, he sent retainers to investigate and discovered the light emanated from the Kobushi Pond in Reimizu Town. They retrieved a rare turtle with red eyes and a white neck from the water. When Tadaaki presented this turtle to the emperor, it was so highly esteemed that the era name was changed to Reiki ("Holy Turtle").
The temple is mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, compiled in {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 16 (797), confirming its long-standing history. Previously known as "Shichiseizan Sokusaiji," it has been a site of devotion to {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bosatsu|1=Myōken}} and was also called "Hitsuji Myōken" (Sheep Myōken). Some scholars link the temple to the {{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tako Kō Monument|1=Tago Stele}}, particularly its reference to "sheep."
During the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|WD=Q2975322|lt=Jōhei-Tengyō War|1=Tengyō no Ran}}, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|en|Taira no Yoshifumi|WD=Q11483528|lt=Taira no Yoshifumi|1=Taira no Yoshifumi}}, cornered in the Battle of Someyagawa, followed a mysterious voice to the temple. There, a monk entrusted him with the "Seven-Star Sword," enabling him to gain the protection of Myōken Bosatsu. Myōken deities revered at {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|en|Chichibu Shrine|WD=Q2963366|lt=Chichibu Shrine|1=Chichibu Shrine}} and {{ill|Chiba Shrine|en|Chiba Shrine|WD=Q11406700|lt=Chiba Shrine|1=Chiba Shrine}} were enshrined from this temple.
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple received a red-seal letter from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, granting it official recognition and support.
<gallery> myokenji2.jpg|Entrance myokenji3.jpg|Someyagawa River myokenji4.jpg|Temple grounds </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * Gunma Prefecture Cultural Properties ** Myōken-sha Main Hall (designated by Takasaki City)
== Access == * Take a bus bound for Gunma Onsen from JR Maebashi Station, and alight at "Myōken-mae."
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasaki.gunma.jp/docs/2013121800907/ Myōken-sha Main Hall] - Takasaki City Official Site
==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}
==References== {{reflist}}
